Description: Asset management and network monitoring systems shall be kept up to date to enable security staff to identify and track down anomalies within networks. This shall include comprehensive details of normal system and traffic behaviour (e.g. source and destination, frequency of communication, protocols and ports used, and expected bandwidth consumed).
VCF Response:
VCF contributes to IT Asset Management programs through built-in inventory, discovery, and integration capabilities that provide the technical foundation for asset tracking and governance.

VCF Operations groups discovered objects logically and provides detailed inventory lists across the environment, covering compute, storage, and networking resources. The inventory management interface supports sorting, searching, and object tagging to organize assets at scale. Custom object groups are containers that collect data from included objects and report on the data collected, allowing administrators to create dashboards and widgets that display resource information for grouped assets. VCF Operations also applies policies to newly discovered objects, such as objects added to an object group.

The Service Discovery adapter automatically discovers applications, services, and their associated metrics (CPU, memory) running on managed infrastructure. Discovery rules can be defined using object types including metrics, relationships, properties, object names, and tag-based criteria. Discovered applications can be exported in CSV format for use in external asset tracking systems.

For organizations using ServiceNow, VCF provides two integration paths. The Management Pack for ServiceNow in VCF Operations populates the ServiceNow CMDB with discovered resources through the ServiceNow Table API and CMDB Meta API, providing CMDB visibility within VCF Operations. VCF Automation also integrates with ServiceNow for ITSM and CMDB synchronization, configurable through extensibility action scripts or through VCF Operations Orchestrator Client hosted workflows. The ServiceNow ITSM Plug-in for VCF Automation provides a structured data source that captures extensive asset metadata, including resource name, operational status, project assignment, storage details, account and region information, endpoint type, zone, environment, and custom properties. The plug-in stores machine resource records in custom ServiceNow CMDB tables that track creation timestamps, deployment IDs, machine IDs, entitled users, and network schema. Deployment records similarly capture blueprint version, project ID, catalog ID, organization ID, operational status, owner, requester, and expiration tracking. The plug-in supports day-2 resource lifecycle actions such as Add Disk, Resize, Power On/Off, Create Snapshot, and Delete, and maintains CMDB accuracy through reconciliation.

VCF Automation adds governance through its project model, which controls who can deploy what resources and where those resources are deployed. Projects link users, catalog items, and IaaS resources together. Organizations in VCF Automation support policy-based governance on resource usage, allowing IT teams to monitor resource consumption, control permissions, and provide managed services with network isolation. Each organization maintains a secure boundary from other organizations. Resource quota policies can be defined through the Policies API to limit resources consumed by each user, project, or organization. Day 2 Action policies restrict which users have access to specific lifecycle operations on deployed resources.

Tags within VCF Automation express capabilities and constraints that define deployments. Cloud administrators can place constraint tags on projects to exercise governance over resource provisioning, supporting asset classification and controlled placement of workloads across the infrastructure.

The broader ITAM program responsibilities of establishing asset governance policies, defining ownership lifecycles, and maintaining organizational accountability remain outside VCF's scope.
VMware vDefend Response:
VMware vDefend (vDefend) supports network security monitoring through multiple integrated capabilities that contribute to enterprise-wide monitoring frameworks.

Security Intelligence, hosted on the Security Services Platform (SSP), provides continuous visibility into network traffic flows across protected workloads. It monitors flows between application pairs, updates the Policy Protection Status for each application to indicate that flows are being monitored, and continuously detects new flows between environment pairs, alerting administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic. The Monitor & Plan Overview page presents a consolidated view of pending actions, data collection activity status, network security posture, flow trends, and a summary of suspicious traffic. The Security Intelligence Recommendations monitoring capability checks for changes in the scope of monitored entities every hour when the Monitoring toggle is enabled, allowing administrators to keep policy recommendations current as environments evolve. Segmentation Monitoring provides dedicated views of application and infrastructure flows with configurable time ranges spanning from the last hour to the last month, and displays flows related to detected infrastructure servers in a specified time interval.

The Network Detection and Response (NDR) Sensor checks the status of activated detection features every 15 minutes and updates its configuration accordingly, supporting sustained threat detection coverage. NDR Sensor health status monitoring tracks whether key components, including network traffic capture, file analysis, and data transmission, are operating properly. The NDR Sensor also sends alerts notifying administrators of conditions such as services being down, high CPU, memory, or disk usage, packet drops, or missed heartbeats. NDR provides role-based access control covering triage operations such as viewing detections and campaigns, as well as SIEM configuration management.

The Security Services Platform monitors its own health, with Yellow or Red health status indicators displayed when issues occur in subcomponents. SSP monitoring tracks the status of the flow clustering job that runs every hour and reports suspicious traffic detector execution status after each run, with values such as SUCCESS, NOT_ENOUGH_BASELINE, and FAILURE.

The Security dashboard provides a consolidated view for monitoring and configuring security features across network workloads. In federated deployments, the Global Manager provides centralized event management and monitoring for malicious IP blocking activity across all Local Managers.

vDefend generates Unified Security Logs that VCF Log Management can collect and analyze. These logs cover Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, Identity Firewall, FQDN Analysis, TLS Inspection, Intrusion Detection/Prevention System (IDPS), and URL Filtering activity. Broader log aggregation and SIEM correlation for enterprise-wide monitoring remain the responsibility of the SIEM and logging infrastructure deployed alongside vDefend.
VCF Protection and Recovery Response:
VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides monitoring capabilities specific to the disaster recovery domain and integrates with the broader VCF monitoring ecosystem through dedicated Protection and Recovery dashboards in VCF Operations. VCF Operations collects configuration, component health, and usage information from protection and recovery servers and site pairs, presenting this through an overview dashboard that displays all site pairs, configurations, health scores, and protection and recoverability status for sites and virtual machines. PNR provides default health alerts for protection groups, recovery plans, cloud file systems, recovery SDDCs, and protected sites through VCF Operations, and the dashboards surface alerts informing administrators about configurations and deployments that require attention. Separate dashboard views cover vSAN Snapshots and Replication monitoring, and an Inventory view provides health status, metrics, alerts, and network topology for all monitored objects.

Within its own domain, PNR provides event logging and configurable alarms for replication and recovery operations. Administrators can create alarms that trigger on specified event conditions, with support for email notifications, SNMP traps, or script execution in response. PNR monitors connections between sites by sending periodic ping requests, logs connection check events, and generates alarms when broken connections are detected. The alarm system generates warning-level alerts for expired snapshot schedules, replication errors such as missing VMs, and connectivity issues with protected and target sites. PNR also monitors resource consumption on the Protection and Recovery Server host and raises alarms when configurable resource thresholds are reached.

PNR includes a health monitoring service that checks each health finding every five minutes and records a timestamp at each check. Health findings are tracked with status history that can be reviewed over configurable time periods, defaulting to a 24-hour window. The Protection and Recovery Appliance supports syslog forwarding to a remote syslog server for log analysis and centralized monitoring, allowing appliance-level events to flow into existing log management infrastructure.

The Clean Room Orchestrator, part of PNR's Cyber Recovery capabilities, supports monitoring of clean room events, tasks, and alarms for recovery operations. Clean Room Orchestrator can forward events to VCF Operations, Microsoft Sentinel, or Splunk Cloud for centralized monitoring, supporting organizations that require DR event data to flow into enterprise SIEM and log management platforms as part of a broader monitoring strategy. The Clean Room Orchestrator integrates with endpoint detection and response tools, including CrowdStrike and Carbon Black sensor appliances, for threat detection within isolated recovery environments.
VMware Data Services Manager Response:
VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to enterprise-wide monitoring controls through a layered set of observability capabilities covering database health, infrastructure compliance, and event-driven alerting.

DSM provides a built-in monitoring framework that tracks database instance health across multiple dimensions. From the Monitoring tab in the DSM UI or the vSphere Client, administrators and authorized users can view real-time and historical metrics for database VMs, including CPU Health, Data Disk Health, System Disk Health, Max Connections, Active Connections, Write Throughput, Read Throughput, Thread Resource Utilization, Slow Queries per Second, InnoDB Buffer Usage, and InnoDB Reads and Writes. The Monitoring page defaults to the last three hours of aggregated metric data, with the time range adjustable through the Time Range Last dropdown. This visibility supports detection of resource contention, performance degradation, and connectivity anomalies at the database layer.

DSM's alert framework provides configurable thresholds for warning and critical conditions on key health indicators. Administrators and users can set separate warning and critical thresholds for CPU Health, Data Disk Health, System Disk Health, and Max Connections parameters. When thresholds are exceeded, DSM generates typed alerts such as Database Engine Health, Custom Config Health, and Backup Storage Health alerts. DSM monitors database replication status and raises an alert when a secondary database is not replicating data from the primary. DSM also raises an alert when metrics are no longer being received from a cluster, supporting detection of monitoring gaps. Alerts can be activated or deactivated per data service through the "Enable Alerts for DB Alert" setting, providing granular control over which databases are subject to active alerting. For each alert, DSM provides mitigation suggestions to help administrators resolve issues without disrupting database services.

In addition to database-level alerts, DSM generates global infrastructure alerts that surface vCenter integration and infrastructure policy issues. These include INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_VC_ALARMS, raised when vCenter resource alarms are active; INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_CLUSTER_COMPLIANT and INVALID_SUPERVISOR_INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_STATUS, which reflect cluster compliance state; VCENTER_CONNECTIVITY, raised when vCenter connectivity fails and triggering troubleshooting steps that include verifying connectivity and checking authentication logs; and LicensingNotUpdated and vCenterBinding API status alerts that surface license and API integration conditions. A Global Alerts page in the DSM UI provides a centralized view of these alerts during and after installation and ongoing operation.

In 9.1, DSM integrates with VCF Operations to extend observability beyond the DSM UI. When configured, VCF Operations automatically discovers and monitors PostgreSQL and MySQL clusters in the Ready state and displays a Data Services View dashboard showing active alerts and inventory items. VCF Operations presents performance data across three levels: cluster-level for overall health, node-level for infrastructure resource use, and database-level for KPIs including Active Connections, Commits, Rollbacks, and Deadlocks. This integration requires configuring the connection between DSM and VCF Operations before use.

DSM also adds SQL Server as a supported database type, with dedicated monitoring through an integrated InfluxDB instance and Telegraf agents deployed across the provider appliance and SQL Server workload clusters. The monitoring framework supports metrics-based and status-condition-based alerts for SQL Server, covering database engine degradation, non-operational status, and automated or transaction log backup failure conditions.

DSM supports event notification to external systems through a webhook integration. Webhooks transmit alert notifications containing the triggered date, triggered time, triggered user email, task status, previous status, and task type. This allows DSM alerts to feed into centralized monitoring or incident management platforms maintained by the organization. DSM generates plug-in registration logs that administrators can review for vCenter connectivity errors, and DSM's troubleshooting guidance directs administrators to review active vCenter infrastructure alarms and resolve underlying issues identified through the alert system.

Enterprise-wide monitoring requires organizational decisions about monitoring architecture, log aggregation platforms, alerting policies, and operational response procedures that lie outside DSM's scope. DSM provides the data and integration points; the organization must determine how those signals are collected, correlated, and acted upon across the broader environment.
VMware Avi Load Balancer Response:
VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) contributes to enterprise-wide monitoring through application-delivery-layer mechanisms built into the Avi Controller and Service Engines.

The Avi Controller monitors Service Engine metrics and sends alarms and notifications to administrators when resource utilization data exceeds defined thresholds. Administrators configure custom thresholds on Service Engine groups through the Resource Events tab in the Avi UI. The Alert feature processes system events and metrics against user-defined rules and generates alerts that trigger configured actions, including sending an email, a syslog message, or an SNMP trap. Alert notifications can be forwarded to remote syslog servers for integration with centralized log management and monitoring platforms.

ControlScripts extend this event-response capability: a ControlScript can alter Avi configuration or communicate to external systems, including through webhook integrations with external monitoring tools, in response to defined system events. This supports dynamic responses including resource scaling or automated notifications based on health scores from monitoring data.

The Avi Controller and Service Engines track health score changes and SSL certificate expiration events. Avi integrates with external monitoring tools such as Prometheus for metrics collection and analysis. User-Defined Metrics allow administrators to define and extract customized telemetry data for specific compliance or reporting needs through the API. Real-time metrics are available via API but deactivate by default after 30 minutes to conserve resources and must be reactivated when sustained real-time visibility is required.

At the data plane, Avi health monitors track the status of backend servers and Service Engines continuously. Service Engine health monitoring records detailed server operational status, including health monitor name, type, last transition timestamps, rise count, fall count, total checks, and total failed checks. Each health monitor can be assigned its own threshold and timeout period for server health validation. Monitors support HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, and UDP health-check protocols with configurable success and failure thresholds, send intervals, and receive timeouts.

In Kubernetes environments managed by the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO), the HealthMonitor custom resource definition (CRD) allows users to define custom health monitoring configurations for backend services with fine-grained control over health check parameters. The Avi Multi-Cluster Kubernetes Operator (AMKO) GlobalDeploymentPolicy supports health monitor references for consistent monitoring across multi-cluster deployments. Within VCF Automation, a HealthMonitor custom resource can create monitors for tracking backend pool member status using TCP, PING, and HTTP check types.

Description: Network changes that could impact network security shall be notified to those monitoring the network. Monitoring processes shall be maintained and modified if necessary.
VCF Response:
VCF provides enterprise-wide monitoring through integrated tools that span infrastructure health, performance metrics, compliance assessment, log analysis, and alerting.

VCF Operations continuously monitors the operational state of all VCF components to detect conditions that may affect availability or compliance. It collects metrics across compute, storage, and networking resources, and correlates them over time to identify trends that may lead to potential failures. VCF Health, a component of VCF Operations, continuously monitors the operational state of VCF components to verify they are functioning as expected, giving platform operators a single view of operational status. Platform operators can configure alerts, perform log analysis, run infrastructure diagnostics, and review VCF health from a unified interface.

VCF Operations provides continuous compliance management by monitoring infrastructure against industry-standard benchmarks and custom policies. Administrators can create custom compliance benchmarks that monitor selected alert definitions and policies, tailoring the monitoring posture to organizational requirements. The platform assesses objects based on selected compliance policies and activates alert definitions associated with the current scorecard, providing ongoing visibility into compliance status. Compliance-related alerts are triggered on objects and displayed through a dedicated compliance alert subtype.

The alerting subsystem in VCF Operations supports the full lifecycle of alert management. Administrators can configure and manage alert definitions to monitor infrastructure events and conditions, including performance metrics, log events, and custom conditions configured through the Management Pack Builder. The Platform Health Alerts page provides a centralized view of all alerts related to overall system health, including alerts from data sources and infrastructure nodes. Health alerts are generated for conditions that affect the health of the environment and require immediate attention. Administrators can create policies to govern how alert definitions are evaluated, including which objects to monitor and what thresholds to apply, and can apply consistent practices to optimize alert behavior across monitored objects.

VCF Operations supports flexible grouping and scheduling to align monitoring with operational realities. Custom groups can be configured with automatic membership criteria to support monitoring strategies and achieve a high level of automation. Maintenance schedules can be assigned to objects undergoing planned downtime to keep monitoring data accurate and avoid false alerts when an object is offline for scheduled maintenance.

VCF Log Management provides centralized log collection and analysis. It ingests logs from VMware vCenter, VMware ESX hosts, NSX, vSAN, VCF Automation, VCF Operations, VCF Identity Broker, VCF Operations for Networks, and VCF Operations HCX. VCF Operations and VCF Log Management must be integrated to implement the centralized log collection architecture. The platform provides dashboards and analytics for troubleshooting and security investigation. Log-based alert definitions allow administrators to trigger notifications when specific log patterns appear across monitored systems. Log forwarding to external SIEM or syslog targets is configurable, with support for TLS encryption to protect log data in transit. The vCenter syslog forwarding API supports TCP, UDP, TLS, and RELP protocols. NSX Manager syslog supports TLS and requires port 6514 for encrypted log transmission.

At the vSphere layer, the alarm infrastructure integrates with events and performance counters to provide real-time alerting. The MetricAlarmExpression data object allows administrators to set alarms on specific performance metrics with configurable monitoring intervals and aggregation functions for processing data points. The vSphere Client provides direct visibility into tasks and events for change tracking and troubleshooting. VCF ships with preconfigured vSphere alarms that are active by default and monitor specific components including the Identity Management Service, Message Bus Configuration Service, ESX Agent Manager, VMware Service Control Agent, vSphere HA host status, and host connection and power state. The vCenter Management Interface provides appliance-level health visibility through an Overall Health badge that reflects one of five states (Good, Warning, Alert, Critical, or Unknown) across all vCenter components, and notifies administrators of available security patches through Critical or Alert badge indicators when automatic patch checks are enabled.

When VCF Automation is integrated with VCF Operations, the Insights dashboard and Alerts tab provide real-time capacity awareness and alert information for VCF Automation objects, giving IT teams visibility into resource usage across both provisioned workloads and the underlying infrastructure. The VCF Automation Monitor tab surfaces deployment health information sourced from VCF Operations, and deployment cards display Last Request Status information that tracks the most recent operation on each deployment (Create, Update, or Delete) and its outcome (Successful, In Progress, or Failed). The Management Pack Builder allows administrators to set up event definitions for integrated systems, monitoring specific conditions or thresholds and configuring alerts for proactive management. Management Packs extend monitoring to network devices, including sensor monitoring for devices such as switches and routers. Adapters can be installed on VCF Operations nodes to extend monitoring to additional systems and data sources beyond the core VCF components.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) layer of VCF extends enterprise monitoring to container workloads and Kubernetes infrastructure. VKS clusters support MachineHealthCheck objects for both control plane nodes and worker node deployments; administrators manage these through the VCF CLI vcf cluster machinehealthcheck command, which supports configuration of unhealthy-condition definitions, maximum unhealthy thresholds (expressed as an absolute number or a percentage), and label selectors to target specific machines. The VCF CLI vcf cluster machinehealthcheck node get and vcf cluster machinehealthcheck control-plane get commands retrieve node and control plane health status on demand. VKS cluster health conditions, including the Ready and ControlPlaneReady status indicators, reflect the operational state of each cluster and are accessible via kubectl and the VCF CLI. The VCF Automation Provider Management UI includes a Services overview interface that exposes VKS cluster management functions and the health status of all cluster elements, giving platform operators a centralized view of the VKS tier. The Kubernetes API server within VKS clusters exposes diagnostic and health check endpoints (/healthz, /livez, /readyz, /statusz, and /metrics) that external monitoring systems can scrape to track API server availability and readiness; the kube-apiserver also emits the kubernetes_healthchecks_total counter and kubernetes_healthcheck gauge metric, which report structured health check status for components including etcd and autoregister-completion. Access to these monitoring endpoints is governed by the built-in Kubernetes system:monitoring RBAC group, which grants read access to liveness, readiness, and metrics endpoints while restricting broader cluster access. The Kubernetes Watch API allows monitoring tools to track changes to any API object as a live stream, supporting event-driven monitoring without continuous polling. The Node Problem Detector monitors node health and surfaces node-level problems as Kubernetes events and node conditions that external monitoring systems can consume via the event stream.

For workload-level monitoring within VKS clusters, Prometheus Operator is available as a standard package and defines custom resource definitions to deploy and manage Prometheus and Alertmanager StatefulSets, enabling high-availability alerting configurations. The Prometheus Alertmanager custom resource definition configures alert grouping, inhibition, and routing to external notification systems. The Istio service mesh, also available as a standard package on VKS clusters, provides service-level monitoring alongside its traffic management capabilities. The Dynatrace Operator can be deployed on VKS clusters to automate the deployment, scaling, and maintenance of the Dynatrace monitoring stack. Historical analysis, dashboarding, and alerting on cluster-level metrics require a dedicated monitoring solution configured to scrape the VKS metrics endpoints and subscribe to the Kubernetes event stream. The vcf cluster support-bundler create command gathers diagnostic information from Supervisor and VKS clusters when troubleshooting is needed.

At the network layer, NSX provides monitored indicators including node uptime status, deployment status, management and controller connections, tunnel status, PNIC status, and uptime duration. NSX time series APIs allow viewing data such as VPN statistics, including metadata about the number of tunnels configured and how many are up or down at a given time.

VCF Operations also supports self-monitoring through the VCF Operations adapter, which collects internal metrics such as activity success counts. This allows administrators to verify that the monitoring platform itself is functioning correctly. The VCF Operations policy engine monitors conditions and generates events to react to changing states in the orchestrator server or connected technologies. The Adapter Instance monitoring interval is configurable, with intervals as granular as five minutes.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) includes a built-in observability stack that supplies monitoring data for AI infrastructure and AI workloads. PAIS Controller metrics are exposed at a Prometheus metrics endpoint on the pais-controller-manager service, allowing collection by monitoring systems and visualization in observability platforms such as Grafana. Observability is activated by updating the PAISConfiguration custom resource with optional sections for Prometheus metrics collection and LLM trace forwarding. The Prometheus runtime can be configured with a specified storage class policy and a configurable metrics retention period, giving administrators control over how long monitoring data is stored. A Prometheus server pod must be in a ready and running state for observability to function.

LLM trace data from PAIS can be forwarded to an OpenTelemetry Collector endpoint over http/protobuf or gRPC, providing distributed tracing for AI model inference activity. The observability.llmTraces configuration governs trace forwarding and works alongside the Prometheus metrics pipeline to produce two signals: metrics for health and performance, and traces for inference-level behavior. PAIS Controller metrics include the Model Endpoint Controller and PAIS Configuration Controller, giving monitoring platforms component-level visibility into PAIS operations.

In 9.1, PAIS adds direct integration with VCF Operations. The controller-pod-metrics-streaming setting activates streaming of PAIS controller pod metrics from the Supervisor cluster to VCF Operations, allowing administrators to view PAIS metrics alongside other VCF infrastructure metrics. A Telegraf deployment in the vmware-system-monitoring namespace uses a telegraf-user-config ConfigMap to manage user-configured monitoring endpoints for PAIS. The VCF Consumption CLI connects to the Supervisor endpoint and enables VI administrators to stream metrics from the PAIS controller pod. DevOps engineers can visualize health and performance metrics for PAIS components running in a VCF Automation namespace using Grafana, and MLOps engineers and application developers can monitor the health, quality, and behavior of AI applications and the models used in agents through the same observability platforms.

PAIS supplies the technical monitoring mechanisms this control requires. Enterprise-wide orchestration of monitoring controls, including alert threshold definition, escalation procedures, and integration with a centralized monitoring or log management platform, remain organizational responsibilities.

These include INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_VC_ALARMS, raised when vCenter resource alarms are active; INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_CLUSTER_COMPLIANT and INVALID_SUPERVISOR_INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_STATUS, which reflect cluster compliance state; VCENTER_CONNECTIVITY, raised when vCenter connectivity fails and triggering troubleshooting steps that include verifying connectivity and checking authentication logs; and LicensingNotUpdated and vCenterBinding API status alerts that surface license and API integration conditions. A Global Alerts page in the DSM UI provides a centralized view of these alerts during and after installation and ongoing operation.

In 9.1, DSM integrates with VCF Operations to extend observability beyond the DSM UI. When configured, VCF Operations automatically discovers and monitors PostgreSQL and MySQL clusters in the Ready state and displays a Data Services View dashboard showing active alerts and inventory items. VCF Operations presents performance data across three levels: cluster-level for overall health, node-level for infrastructure resource use, and database-level for KPIs including Active Connections, Commits, Rollbacks, and Deadlocks. This integration requires configuring the connection between DSM and VCF Operations before use.

DSM also adds SQL Server as a supported database type, with dedicated monitoring through an integrated InfluxDB instance and Telegraf agents deployed across the provider appliance and SQL Server workload clusters. The monitoring framework supports metrics-based and status-condition-based alerts for SQL Server, covering database engine degradation, non-operational status, and automated or transaction log backup failure conditions.

DSM supports event notification to external systems through a webhook integration. Webhooks transmit alert notifications containing the triggered date, triggered time, triggered user email, task status, previous status, and task type. This allows DSM alerts to feed into centralized monitoring or incident management platforms maintained by the organization. DSM generates plug-in registration logs that administrators can review for vCenter connectivity errors, and DSM's troubleshooting guidance directs administrators to review active vCenter infrastructure alarms and resolve underlying issues identified through the alert system.

Enterprise-wide monitoring requires organizational decisions about monitoring architecture, log aggregation platforms, alerting policies, and operational response procedures that lie outside DSM's scope. DSM provides the data and integration points; the organization must determine how those signals are collected, correlated, and acted upon across the broader environment.
VMware Avi Load Balancer Response:
VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) contributes to enterprise-wide monitoring through application-delivery-layer mechanisms built into the Avi Controller and Service Engines.

The Avi Controller monitors Service Engine metrics and sends alarms and notifications to administrators when resource utilization data exceeds defined thresholds. Administrators configure custom thresholds on Service Engine groups through the Resource Events tab in the Avi UI. The Alert feature processes system events and metrics against user-defined rules and generates alerts that trigger configured actions, including sending an email, a syslog message, or an SNMP trap. Alert notifications can be forwarded to remote syslog servers for integration with centralized log management and monitoring platforms.

ControlScripts extend this event-response capability: a ControlScript can alter Avi configuration or communicate to external systems, including through webhook integrations with external monitoring tools, in response to defined system events. This supports dynamic responses including resource scaling or automated notifications based on health scores from monitoring data.

The Avi Controller and Service Engines track health score changes and SSL certificate expiration events. Avi integrates with external monitoring tools such as Prometheus for metrics collection and analysis. User-Defined Metrics allow administrators to define and extract customized telemetry data for specific compliance or reporting needs through the API. Real-time metrics are available via API but deactivate by default after 30 minutes to conserve resources and must be reactivated when sustained real-time visibility is required.

At the data plane, Avi health monitors track the status of backend servers and Service Engines continuously. Service Engine health monitoring records detailed server operational status, including health monitor name, type, last transition timestamps, rise count, fall count, total checks, and total failed checks. Each health monitor can be assigned its own threshold and timeout period for server health validation. Monitors support HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, and UDP health-check protocols with configurable success and failure thresholds, send intervals, and receive timeouts.

In Kubernetes environments managed by the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO), the HealthMonitor custom resource definition (CRD) allows users to define custom health monitoring configurations for backend services with fine-grained control over health check parameters. The Avi Multi-Cluster Kubernetes Operator (AMKO) GlobalDeploymentPolicy supports health monitor references for consistent monitoring across multi-cluster deployments. Within VCF Automation, a HealthMonitor custom resource can create monitors for tracking backend pool member status using TCP, PING, and HTTP check types.

Description: Providers shall monitor physical and logical interfaces between networks that operate at different trust levels, as well as between groups of network functions (e.g. core networks and access networks).
VCF Response:
VCF provides several capabilities for monitoring inbound and outbound communications traffic across the software-defined data center, though continuous monitoring for unusual or unauthorized activities also requires organizational processes, defined baselines, and integration with external security analytics tools.

NSX, the networking component of VCF, provides Live Traffic Analysis (LTA), which monitors live traffic at a source or between a source and destination. LTA includes bi-directional packet tracing and packet capture capabilities that can identify packet drops and bad flows between endpoints. In addition to LTA, NSX supports traceflow for inspecting the path of a packet through the network, allowing both Enterprise Admins and Project Admins to trace packet paths within their scope. NSX also supports IPFIX-based flow monitoring, which enables network administrators to gain visibility into traffic traversing the network by exporting flow records to external collectors for analysis. NSX provides flow query capabilities that support filtering and analysis of VTEP traffic, management traffic, and extended Layer 2 network traffic. NSX App IDs can classify traffic under proxy configurations, supporting traffic identification workflows. At the VMware ESX host level, the pktcap-uw utility supports packet capture at multiple points in the host network stack, including monitoring traffic arriving at physical adapters and specifying capture direction with the --capture and --dir options. The vSphere Distributed Switch also supports NetFlow monitoring, configurable on individual distributed ports and port groups, which provides flow-level visibility into IP traffic traversing the virtual switch fabric.

VCF Operations and VCF Operations for Networks extend monitoring with metrics, dashboards, and alerting. VCF Operations collects network metrics including net|allInboundTotal and net|allOutboundTotal, which track all inbound and outbound connections respectively, along with TCP inbound and outbound connection metrics. The Logical Switch Inbound and Outbound Throughput dashboard monitors traffic throughput for logical switches, helping identify potential bottlenecks or anomalous traffic patterns. The Network Operations application can display the top 25 applications based on incoming traffic, outgoing traffic, critical problems, and failed intents, giving operators a prioritized view of traffic activity across the environment. Port channel metrics measure discarded inbound and outbound packets, dropped received packets, and dropped transmitted packets, providing indicators of network issues or potential abuse.

VCF Operations for Networks provides outlier detection with configurable traffic direction selection supporting outgoing, incoming, or both directions. It also performs intent-based verification, modeling all possible network-wide behavior every 30 minutes and triggering alerts when an intent is violated. VPC Flow Logs capture information about IP traffic going to and from network interfaces, providing additional visibility into virtual network traffic patterns. VCF Operations provides an overview of network traffic along with a list of all alerts associated with SDDC entities, consolidating network alert information into a single view. VCF Operations also generates informational alerts for security-relevant network conditions, such as when a distributed firewall configuration allows all traffic by default.

Within VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters, which form the native Kubernetes consumption layer of VCF, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources define the authorized ingress and egress communications for each pod, scoped by namespace, pod selector, IP block, and port. Ingress rules specify which sources and ports may reach a given set of pods; egress rules specify which destinations and ports those pods may contact, including IP block restrictions via the egress.to.ipBlock field. When Antrea is the CNI, the AntreaNetworkPolicyStats feature can be enabled to capture per-policy enforcement statistics, giving operators a continuous record of which policies are matching traffic and how frequently. VKS ClusterClass variables allow administrators to define firewall inbound rules at the cluster level, specifying protocol by IANA number or alias (such as 'tcp' or 'udp'), source IP address or CIDR block, and port ranges via fromPort and toPort fields. Istio, available as a Standard Package for VKS clusters, automatically generates metrics, logs, and traces for all traffic within the cluster, including cluster ingress and egress; its tracing subsystem can export distributed traces to OpenTelemetry-compatible backends such as Jaeger or Zipkin, providing visibility into inter-service communications. The Contour ingress controller, also available as a Standard Package, establishes a defined entry point for inbound HTTP(S) traffic via an Envoy LoadBalancer service that receives an external IP address from the Workload Network Ingress CIDR range, centralizing the point at which inbound cluster traffic is observed and controlled. Organizations deploying VKS clusters can also integrate Wiz, which continuously monitors the cloud environment and deployed workloads for security risks and compliance deviations.

VCF Operations supports outbound alerting through configurable plug-in instances. Administrators can configure notification rules that filter alerts sent to external systems via Standard Email, REST, SNMP Trap, or Log File outbound plug-ins. An HTTP proxy can be configured for outbound communication over HTTP/HTTPS, and once configured, all corresponding outbound HTTP(S) communication routes through that proxy. Outbound plug-in instances can be activated or deactivated to start or stop sending messages without removing the configuration from the environment.

These capabilities provide the technical foundation for monitoring inbound and outbound communications traffic across the infrastructure, networking, and workload layers of VCF. However, continuously monitoring for unusual or unauthorized activities requires the organization to define traffic baselines, configure alert thresholds, establish monitoring procedures, and integrate VCF telemetry with a security information and event management (SIEM) platform or similar analytics tool that can correlate traffic data and detect anomalies over time. This telemetry spans NSX flow records and IPFIX exports, VCF Operations network metrics, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy enforcement statistics, and Istio service mesh traces. VMware vDefend, a separate product that extends NSX with security functions, adds distributed firewall connection and packet logging, stateful flow tracking with IPFIX export, deep packet inspection, IDS/IPS, TLS inspection, and microsegmentation, which together provide the security-oriented detection of unusual or unauthorized traffic that VCF alone does not.
VMware vDefend Response:
VMware vDefend provides multiple capabilities for continuously monitoring inbound and outbound communications traffic for unusual or unauthorized activities.

Security Intelligence continuously monitors for new flows between environments and alerts administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic, enabling ongoing policy adjustments. The Environments Monitoring dashboard provides traffic flow analysis with protection status summaries for selected environment pairs within a configurable time range. Private IP range definitions establish the boundary between internal and external workloads, so traffic crossing that boundary is treated as external and subject to additional scrutiny. The Secure External Communications security project identifies workloads with inbound or outbound connections to external IP addresses, helping address exposure to external threats. The Monitor and Plan Overview page provides a consolidated view of pending actions, data collection status, network security posture, flow trends, and a summary of suspicious traffic. Network traffic flows in Security Intelligence are based on the distributed firewall rules in effect and on traffic flows observed during the selected time period, including traffic that matches stateful Layer 4 DFW rules.

Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) for Suspicious Traffic continuously monitors and analyzes network traffic and flow records using advanced machine learning models and statistical techniques to establish a baseline of normal network behavior and identify deviations such as protocol misuse and unusual traffic patterns. NTA detects anomalies in the time series profile of individual hosts, identifying unusual network traffic patterns that may indicate compromise or policy violation. Suspicious traffic detectors report execution status after each run, with possible values of NOT_STARTED, SUCCESS, NOT_ENOUGH_BASELINE, or FAILURE, giving administrators visibility into detector health and data availability. Suspicious traffic events are surfaced through the Suspicious Traffic Events dashboard and the Threat Monitoring tab within Security Services Platform, providing a centralized view for security analysts.

vDefend IDS/IPS inspects communications traffic for known attack signatures and suspicious behavior across both distributed and gateway deployments. IDS/IPS event monitoring supports filtering by traffic type, allowing administrators to focus on distributed traffic, gateway traffic, or both. Threshold and rate filters allow event triggering based on traffic behavior patterns, enabling administrators to tune detection sensitivity to their environment.

The vDefend distributed firewall supports Monitoring Mode, which enables logging for all connectivity strategies to audit traffic flowing through the environment. DFW rules support per-rule logging of both allowed and dropped traffic. The Security Overview Dashboard provides centralized visibility into threat events, with filtering by All Traffic, Distributed Only, or Gateway Only traffic types.

The vDefend gateway firewall secures north-south traffic at the environment boundary, providing a control point for monitoring and filtering inbound and outbound communications. Geo-Fencing capabilities allow administrators to create rules that monitor and control traffic based on geographic origin or destination, down to the country level. In multi-tenant environments, the Centralized Transit Gateway Firewall enables project administrators and providers to configure firewall rules on Transit Gateway nodes for managing incoming and outgoing traffic to external networks.
VCF Protection and Recovery Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.
VMware Data Services Manager Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.
VMware Avi Load Balancer Response:
VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides several mechanisms for continuous monitoring of application-layer communications traffic flowing through its configured virtual services.

At the analytics layer, Avi Analytics continuously evaluates traffic against statistical baselines for each virtual service, detecting anomalies when traffic falls outside the statistical norm for the time period. The system assigns anomaly penalties for deviations and surfaces them in virtual service and pool analytics dashboards. Attack Visibility tools track over 500 real-time metrics across virtual services, supporting DDoS detection and response workflows.

DataScripts extend the monitoring and mitigation capabilities by allowing custom identification of suspicious traffic and execution of appropriate mitigation actions at both L4 and L7 layers. Port-scan detection allows Avi Load Balancer to report or block sources performing port scans as part of DDoS mitigation.

Bot Management via Avi WAF bot detection can classify traffic as dangerous bots and apply HTTP security actions including closing connections. In Cisco ACI-based deployments, integrated WAF (iWAF) provides granular security insights on traffic flows and rule matches, supporting precise policy enforcement based on observed traffic patterns.

For integration with external monitoring infrastructure, the Virtual Service Sideband Profile replicates incoming HTTP(S) traffic at the protocol level to designated logging or sideband servers. This enables deep traffic inspection by external SIEM platforms or inspection appliances. Organizations should configure log forwarding to a centralized syslog or SIEM target to aggregate Avi's monitoring output with broader network telemetry and to satisfy continuous monitoring requirements across all communications paths, including those not routed through Avi virtual services.

Avi's monitoring scope is bounded to traffic flowing through its configured virtual services. Communications that bypass Avi, such as east-west workload traffic not routed through the load balancer, require separate monitoring tooling at the platform level.

Description: Systems that collect and process logging and monitoring data shall be treated as network oversight functions.
VCF Response:
VCF separates security functions from non-security functions through architectural isolation at the platform, network, virtual machine, administrative, and containerized workload layers.

The management domain/workload domain architecture is the primary mechanism for this separation. The management domain hosts all security-critical management components: VCF Operations, VMware vCenter, NSX Manager, and VCF Log Management. Security services such as identity management (VCF SSO), certificate authority functions, and platform configuration run within the management domain, architecturally isolated from tenant workload virtual machines. Workload domains are deployed separately to run customer workloads, each with its own isolated pool of compute, network, and storage resources. Workload domains cannot share the management domain's NSX cluster. This structural boundary keeps security management infrastructure separate from the workloads it governs, and means that security-critical services are not co-resident with general-purpose workloads.

Administrative separation reinforces this architectural split. VCF implements role-based access control across all components, with distinct roles for security functions. Cryptographic operations are gated by specific privileges; only users assigned Cryptographic Operations privileges can encrypt or decrypt virtual machines. The platform provides a No Cryptography Administrator system role that supports all Administrator privileges except for Cryptographic Operations, and administrators can clone this role to create custom roles that grant only a subset of cryptographic capabilities (such as allowing encryption but not decryption). This isolates cryptographic key management and encryption functions from general administrative activities, requiring explicit privilege grants before any user can interact with the platform's cryptographic subsystem. The vCenter authorization model allows consistent security control regardless of operational context.

NSX RBAC separates security administration from network operations with dedicated Security Admin and Security Operator roles scoped to individual projects. These roles have RBAC permissions only within the scope of the project, not for the entire NSX system. NSX also supports creation of custom roles to suit specific operational requirements. VCF Automation restricts infrastructure-level resources to the Assembler Administrator role, which is the only role that can add cloud accounts, create new projects, and assign project administrators. Project creation is restricted to the Assembler/Service Broker Administrator role only. This keeps security configuration and infrastructure provisioning out of reach of general users.

At the virtual machine level, VCF's Security Configuration Guide defines isolation parameters that restrict VM interactions with host-level security functions. On supported Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP hardware, Confidential Compute strengthens this boundary by encrypting virtual machine memory in hardware, reducing exposure of in-use workload memory to the hypervisor and to host-level functions when configured correctly. The isolation.device.edit.disable setting restricts unauthorized modification of virtual device configurations, and the isolation.tools.getCreds.disable setting restricts access to credential retrieval functions. These controls enforce a boundary between guest operating system operations and host-level security mechanisms, so that workloads running inside VMs cannot reach into the hypervisor's security layer. The isolation.tools.setinfo.disable parameter can also be configured to restrict information disclosure from guest processes to the hypervisor layer. For Windows virtual machines, vSphere supports Virtualization-based Security (VBS), which uses hardware-assisted isolation to protect key security services within the guest; the Credential Guard feature within VBS isolates and hardens key system and user secrets against compromise.

At the network layer, NSX Projects isolate security and networking objects across tenants in a single NSX deployment. They isolate networking and security configurations for multiple departments or organizational units, so security policies for one group of workloads do not interfere with another and remain separated from the platform's own security infrastructure. In VCF Automation, security groups provide network isolation for deployed resources. Security groups define isolation settings for on-demand private or outbound networks, and network profiles support isolation policies defined through on-demand or existing security groups with associated firewall rules. On-demand security groups carry pre-configured firewall rules that enforce isolation boundaries independently of the general network topology. This layered approach separates security policy enforcement from general workload provisioning and network traffic management.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor extend this isolation discipline to containerized workloads. The Kubernetes control plane, including the API server, etcd data store, scheduler, and admission controllers, runs as dedicated infrastructure separate from tenant workloads. Kubernetes authorization, enforced at the API server, restricts each workload's access to its own API resources and not those of other tenants. Admission controllers, including validating and mutating webhooks, intercept requests within this control-plane layer before any workload action takes effect. Pod Security Admission (PSA) adds a further layer enforced entirely at the API server, prior to any workload execution: VKS supports Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted PSA profiles, and the Restricted profile governs whether containers run as root, allow privilege escalation, access host namespaces, or configure seccomp profiles, enforcing pod hardening standards independently of the workloads it governs.

At the kernel level, Linux security modules, including seccomp, AppArmor, and SELinux, provide security restrictions that operate below the container runtime and are applied through SecurityContext configuration in Kubernetes. Seccomp restricts the Linux kernel syscall attack surface available inside containers using BPF-based allow or deny profiles; AppArmor provides runtime security restrictions on Linux nodes. On Windows nodes, Windows overlay networks provide isolation of container networks from the host underlay, keeping containerized workload traffic separate from the host networking layer. The Supervisor secret injection agent container enforces a RuntimeDefault seccomp profile to restrict its own system calls, applying kernel-level isolation to security infrastructure components themselves. Kubernetes namespaces isolate each workload's ConfigMaps and Secrets, allowing security policies to be scoped and tailored per workload. The Secret Store Supervisor Service extends this by tying secrets to a single Supervisor namespace, restricting access to VM Service VMs, vSphere Pods, and VKS cluster pods in the same namespace, and enforcing role-based access control across administrator, DevOps engineer, and application tenant roles. Kubernetes RBAC enforces least-privilege access to Secret objects as a complementary mechanism.

At the node level, node isolation lowers the risk of information disclosure: an attacker that escapes from a container has access only to containers and volumes mounted to that node. Node label keys used for isolation policies are chosen to resist unauthorized modification. Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) for vSphere Supervisor and Kubernetes Releases (VKrs) are available through Tanzu STIG Hardening, providing hardening configuration requirements that formalize the separation of security functions from general workload configuration in the Consumption layer.

VCF Operations provides dedicated security monitoring capabilities that are separated from general operational monitoring. The Security Operations function provides visibility into security across user security and component security for VMs, ESX hosts, and vSAN. The Compliance capability surfaces security posture information in a dedicated view alongside the security dashboard. This functional separation allows security teams to monitor and assess security state through purpose-built interfaces without mixing it with performance or capacity data, and without requiring access to general infrastructure management tools.
VMware vDefend Response:
VMware vDefend (vDefend) contributes to isolating security functions from non-security functions through network-layer segmentation and policy enforcement mechanisms that create technically enforceable boundaries between security-critical infrastructure and general workloads.

The vDefend distributed firewall (DFW) enforces microsegmentation at the virtual network interface card (vNIC) level, applying firewall policy to east-west traffic between virtual machines. Security infrastructure such as management appliances, logging collectors, and monitoring endpoints can be placed behind DFW rules that restrict access to only authorized administrative traffic, creating a network-level boundary between management zones and workload zones. vDefend introduces five system-defined VPC security strategies: VPC Isolation, VPC Isolation with Essential Services, VPC External Connectivity, VPC Secure Connection, and NONE. These strategies give administrators a structured set of isolation postures that can be applied directly to VPCs through DFW, establishing consistent security baselines without requiring administrators to construct common isolation patterns from scratch.

Centralized Transit Gateway Firewalls, available in 9.1, extend isolation to project-level boundaries in multi-tenant VCF environments. Enterprise administrators can enforce Isolation with Essential Services, denying all inter-project communication except for infrastructure protocols such as DHCP, DNS, NTP, and ICMP, or enforce Enterprise Gateway Isolation, which denies all communication between distinct projects. These Gateway Firewall controls allow security-focused projects to be separated from general-purpose workloads at the transit layer.

Privileged labels provide a tamper-proof mechanism for enforcing security policies by anchoring firewall rules to immutable labels that prevent virtual machines from escaping security groups. Administrators define privileged labels within an organization space, and DFW rules anchored to those labels remain in force even if workload configurations change, reducing the risk that a security boundary is bypassed through group membership manipulation. DFW governance further supports isolation integrity by discouraging overly permissive rules such as ANY/ANY/ANY ALLOW.

Security Services Platform (SSP) provides environment-based segmentation through block policies that restrict traffic between environment pairs such as Production, Development, and Test. Environment Protection assessments measure the percentage of environment pairs operating in blocked mode and the percentage of compute resources covered by environment-specific segmentation policies, giving administrators quantitative visibility into isolation coverage. Security Intelligence generates granular application-level rules allowing only authorized traffic between environments. SSP guidance calls for identifying and securing all inter-environment flows before enforcing strict block rules, so that isolation is applied with full awareness of legitimate traffic paths.

Bare Metal Security extends DFW-based isolation to physical servers. Security policies are defined, propagated, and enforced across bare metal servers through SSP, applying Layer 3/4 DFW policy to control incoming and outgoing traffic on physical infrastructure. SSP role-based access control (RBAC) defines roles including Security Engineer, Security Operator, and Support Bundle Collector, each with specific responsibilities and permission levels, which restricts access to the security management plane itself and maintains the integrity of the enforcement layer.
VCF Protection and Recovery Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.
VMware Data Services Manager Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.
VMware Avi Load Balancer Response:
VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides multiple mechanisms to isolate security-oriented functions from general application traffic and administrative operations at the application delivery layer.

At the network interface level, the Avi Controller supports a dedicated secondary interface using the SE_SECURE_CHANNEL label, which separates Service Engine communication traffic from management traffic. Assigning this label to a secondary interface allows different physical or logical interfaces to carry different traffic types, supporting isolation of sensitive control channels from general management network traffic.

At the data plane level, Service Engine Groups are the primary mechanism for isolating application workloads with different security profiles. Applications deployed on separate Service Engine Groups receive guaranteed data plane isolation, with each group maintaining its own pool of Service Engine instances. Capacity from one group cannot be drawn from another, creating boundaries between application workloads with different security requirements.

The Avi tenancy model provides two levels of isolation. Tenants configured in Provider Context provide control plane isolation only, preventing administrative namespaces from overlapping across tenants. Tenants configured in Tenant Context provide both control plane and data plane isolation, making Tenant Context the stronger isolation boundary for multi-tenant deployments where data plane separation is required. Service Engine Groups independently provide data plane isolation regardless of tenant context. In VCF environments, Service Engine Group allocation in VCF Automation blueprints supports operational isolation by keeping each project within its designated boundaries.

At the access control layer, role-based access control (RBAC) through Auth Mapping Profiles maps user groups to specific roles with tenant-scoped access, supporting isolation of administrative functions across organizational boundaries. Auth Mapping rules can be configured to enforce tenant isolation for all users except designated super users who require cross-tenant access, and can also restrict specific users from obtaining any roles or tenant assignments upon login.

In Kubernetes deployments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) integrates with the Avi Web Application Firewall (WAF) to provide application-layer security isolation in VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) environments. This allows WAF policies to be applied to application workloads at the ingress layer, separating L7 security enforcement from the underlying network fabric.

Effective isolation at the hypervisor and host layers, including compute separation between Service Engine VMs, relies on VCF platform mechanisms (see VCF Coverage). Customers must configure separate Service Engine Group assignments and appropriate tenant context to activate data plane isolation, as the default deployment mode does not enforce these boundaries automatically.

Description: The integrity of logging data shall be protected, and any modification alerted and attributed.
VCF Response:
VCF protects sensitive and regulated data in log files through a layered approach that spans design philosophy, access controls, data masking, centralized log management, and cryptographic protections. The Consumption layer, including VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), vSphere namespaces, and the Kubernetes control plane, extends these protections to workload infrastructure running on the VCF platform.

At the hypervisor layer, VMware ESX log files are not encrypted at rest because they are designed not to contain sensitive data such as passwords or encryption keys. This design principle reduces the risk of sensitive data exposure in host-level logs by avoiding the problem at the source. The platform documentation acknowledges that core files and audit logs may contain sensitive information, which drives the design of access restrictions and handling procedures around log artifacts. When support bundles or core files are collected, administrators should be aware that these artifacts might contain sensitive information and handle them accordingly. vSphere also supports encrypting Fault Tolerance log traffic to reduce exposure of sensitive virtual machine state data during replication operations. This is configured via the Encrypted FT option in VM Options; placing Fault Tolerance logging traffic on a dedicated private network provides additional network-level isolation.

VCF Operations for Networks includes a control that allows Admin role users to view and enable or disable Personally Identifiable Information (PII) in audit logs. This capability gives administrators direct control over whether sensitive identity data is recorded in log output, reducing exposure of regulated data at the point of generation.

At the Kubernetes API server layer, VKS clusters support a configurable audit policy that determines what data appears in API server audit logs. Audit policy rules are defined in a configuration file and support four logging levels: None (no event recorded), Metadata (request metadata only, without request or response bodies), Request (request metadata and body), and RequestResponse (full metadata, request, and response bodies). Applying the Metadata level to sensitive resource types such as Secrets and ConfigMaps allows administrators to capture meaningful audit trails while keeping the contents of sensitive resources out of log records. Audit log files are written to a protected path on the control plane node using the --audit-log-path flag, and the Kubernetes audit logger records all actions taken by the API for later analysis in the event of a security incident.

Access to log data is controlled through role-based access at multiple levels. VCF Log Management requires permissions to be granted to VCF SSO users and groups before they can access the instance, restricting who can view and search log data. VCF Operations provides role-based access to audit data, restricting who can view users, objects, and collected information. Auditing capabilities are designed to meet audit requirements for business-critical applications that contain sensitive data. Audit data can be filtered by User ID, User Name, Auth Source, Session, Client IP, Category, and Message, allowing administrators to scope access to log content and limit exposure of sensitive fields. Event auditing registers each platform interaction, increasing user accountability by tracking who accessed or modified log-related configurations. At the Kubernetes layer, access to log data through the API, such as retrieving pod logs with kubectl logs or querying cluster events, is governed by Kubernetes RBAC. ClusterRoles and custom roles restrict which principals are permitted to retrieve log output, reducing the exposure of sensitive data that workloads write to standard output. Audit log files on control plane node filesystem paths are subject to host operating system file permissions.

Centralized log collection through VCF Operations reduces the number of locations where sensitive log data resides. Rather than leaving application log messages distributed across local file systems on individual components, VCF Log Management aggregates log sources into a managed environment. The VCF Log Management UI supports log source configuration and partitioning, which allows administrators to isolate sensitive log streams from general operational data. For workloads running in vSphere namespaces, the LogAgentConfiguration Custom Resource can be applied to a namespace to stream syslog messages from vSphere Pods to a remote syslog server; each forwarded message carries structured metadata fields including namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name. On VKS clusters, Fluent Bit logging agents deployed in the vmware-system-logging namespace collect and forward logs from cluster components. Fluent Bit tags Kubernetes API server audit logs separately from container logs and Linux daemon logs, which allows different forwarding, access control, and retention policies to be applied to audit data; Fluent Bit supports forwarding to VCF Log Management, Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk.

For log data in transit, VCF supports forwarding logs to external syslog targets using TLS encryption. NSX Manager syslog supports the TLS protocol and requires port 6514 to be open on both the NSX Manager and the remote log server for encrypted log transmission. Fluent Bit on VKS clusters supports TLS for log forwarding to syslog targets. When forwarding to VCF Log Management, Fluent Bit connects via CFAPI on port 9543 for TLS-encrypted connections or port 9000 for non-TLS connections. For environments that require validated cryptographic protections, VCF Log Management supports FIPS Compliance Mode as a deployment option. When enabled, this mode applies FIPS-validated cryptographic algorithms to data handling within the log management infrastructure. Kubernetes documentation recommends deploying cryptographic protections in high-assurance environments to help keep logs tamper-resistant and confidential.

Log retention and lifecycle management help reduce exposure of any inadvertently logged sensitive data. VCF Operations allows administrators to edit log file folders, limit retained log size, and set logging levels. The log.keepOld parameter controls how many log files are retained, and administrators can limit log file size on virtual machines. By default, ESX log files are stored in an in-memory file system and are lost when the host reboots; configuring persistent logging to a datastore preserves audit log continuity across host restarts and reduces the risk of losing sensitive audit data. These controls reduce the window during which sensitive data remains on disk.

NSX audit log messages include a structured data field (audit="true") that distinguishes audit records from operational logs, enabling targeted retention and access policies for audit-sensitive content.

Pod Security Admission (PSA) on VKS clusters provides an additional mechanism for generating security-relevant audit annotations. In PSA audit mode, security policy violations trigger an audit annotation added to the event recorded in the Kubernetes API server audit log without blocking the workload. This allows administrators to identify workloads that violate Pod security standards while maintaining operations, and those audit annotations can be captured and forwarded through the Fluent Bit pipeline for centralized review.

Kubernetes documentation recommends that applications running on VKS avoid logging Secret values in the clear after reading them from environment variables or mounted volumes, so that credentials and other regulated data do not appear in container standard output or standard error streams. This is design-time guidance rather than a platform enforcement mechanism; organizations deploying workloads on VKS are responsible for implementing log sanitization at the application level.

Defining what counts as sensitive log content in the organization's context, configuring the toggles per log destination, operating access policy on log management infrastructure, defining and enforcing Kubernetes audit policy rules for sensitive resource types, configuring application workloads to avoid logging credentials or regulated data, and verifying that log storage systems apply appropriate access controls and encryption at rest sit with the organization.
VMware vDefend Response:
VMware vDefend protects sensitive and regulated data in its log files through TLS-encrypted log forwarding, structured log content, and integration with centralized log management.

For log data in transit, the Malware Prevention Service VM can be configured to forward log messages to a remote logging server using TLS with mutual certificate authentication. The configuration accepts logging server IP address, protocol, log level, server CA certificate, client CA certificate, client certificate, and client key. The Malware Prevention Service VM currently supports only the SECURITY message ID for log filtering when transmitting to a remote server, which limits the log categories forwarded.

Malware Prevention Service event logs record a detailed set of file analysis lifecycle events: file intercepted, verdict cache hit, file sent for local static analysis, file sent for cloud dynamic analysis, verdict obtained, and policy enforced. Each entry includes structured JSON attributes covering file hashes (SHA256, SHA1, MD5), file metadata (fileName, fileType, fileSize), malware classification, malware family, error codes, HTTP domain, HTTP method, user agent, file upload status, submission status, verdict classification (such as BENIGN), and analysis score. Given the sensitivity of this data, access to these logs at the receiving system should be restricted to authorized personnel.

VMware vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS can send events to external syslog consumers by enabling the ids_events_to_syslog setting on an IDS/IPS profile; the setting is disabled by default. IDS/IPS logs include the user ID whose traffic generated the event, providing identity context alongside detection metadata. vDefend components write to log files in /var/log, with syslog messages conforming to RFC 5424 on vDefend appliances and RFC 3164 on ESX hosts. Both NSX Manager and ESX hosts must be configured to forward syslog to the central log repository for complete IDS/IPS event coverage.

Network Detection and Response supports SSL verification for event logs sent over HTTPS, protecting detection event data in transit when logs are forwarded to an external receiving system.

Identity Firewall Event Log Sources can be configured to collect logs from Active Directory Event Log Sources or from VCF Log Management. VCF Log Management provides a centralized log management framework for vDefend; logs are forwarded by configuring VCF Log Management as a syslog server, where centralized access controls, retention policies, and enterprise-wide log governance can be applied.

While vDefend provides TLS transport protection for Malware Prevention logs, IDS/IPS event forwarding with identity context, SSL-verified transport in Network Detection and Response, and integration paths to centralized logging, the broader log protection framework including enterprise-wide retention policies, centralized access governance, and log access controls at the receiving system requires VCF platform components and organizational processes outside vDefend's scope.
VCF Protection and Recovery Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.
VMware Data Services Manager Response:
VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) addresses this control through a combination of by-design log content restrictions, access controls, log storage options, and database-engine-level audit configurations that reduce the exposure of sensitive data in audit and operational log files.

DSM audit logs are structured around a defined set of fields: Component, Event Type, Event Details, Event Time (UTC), Status, and Source. The Source field records either the initiating user's email identifier or "System" for automated operations. Gateway audit log files contain standard Kubernetes audit events, and the structured field schema limits what data appears in log records by design. This design is documented under Audit Logs in Data Services Manager in the DSM documentation.

Access to audit log data within DSM is controlled through the role-based access model. The System Audit Events view in the Provider interface is the designated location for reviewing non-database-related operations and tasks; access is scoped to authenticated DSM sessions. Audit events can be exported in CSV or XLSX format through the ACTIONS drop-down in the System Audit Events view, and exports reflect events visible to the requesting user based on their role.

Gateway audit log files are stored on the Provider VM at /var/log/tdm/provider/audit/ with filenames in the format gateway-audit-timestamp.log. Access to these files is governed by the Provider VM's operating system controls. DSM can be configured to archive gateway audit log files periodically to an S3-compatible repository using the Provider Log Repo Url setting under Managing Storage Settings, which allows organizations to consolidate audit logs in a separately protected storage system. DSM also supports log forwarding, configurable through the log-forwarding setting in Managing Storage Settings, allowing logs to be sent to a central log aggregation system such as a syslog-compatible platform where additional access restrictions can be applied.

For SQL Server databases, DSM provides additional audit logging controls at the engine level. When creating a SQL Server cluster, administrators can configure Login Auditing to select the level of audit logging for system login events and set a log retention period. DSM uses server-level DDL triggers to restrict the file paths available for SQL Server audit logs, limiting where those logs can be written. The errorLog.numErrorLogs engine setting controls how many error log files are retained before recycling.

DSM restricts what is logged and provides mechanisms to forward and archive logs. Organizations are responsible for implementing access controls on the S3 log repository, configuring the receiving log aggregation system with appropriate restrictions, applying log retention and integrity policies, and reviewing log contents to confirm that no application-level data patterns create inadvertent sensitive data exposure in event detail fields.
VMware Avi Load Balancer Response:
VMware Avi Load Balancer includes a Sensitive Log Profile (sensitive_log_profile) that masks or removes sensitive data from application-layer client logs on a per-virtual-service basis.
